intro: C++ focuses on absolute hardware optimization and explicit resource lifetimes through RAII, introducing significant syntax complexity to achieve low-level performance. Ruby prioritizes developer productivity and code readability by providing a fully object-oriented, dynamic scripting runtime environment. Transitioning components from C++ to Ruby trades raw compilation performance for rapid application prototyping, clean text parsing, and highly flexible runtime metaprogramming.

diffs.from: Static, nominal type layout requiring explicit compile-time resolution, templates, and strict casting rules.
diffs.to: Dynamic, strong type model relying completely on late runtime method dispatch and duck typing conventions.

diffs.from: Dense, bracket-delimited structure utilizing namespaces, pointer markers, and separate declarations.
diffs.to: Clean, highly expressive layout omitting parenthetical requirements and explicitly terminating blocks via `end` keywords.

diffs.from: Meticulous manual or RAII-driven stack and heap tracking utilizing deterministic smart pointer lifecycles.
diffs.to: Automated memory tracking governed by an internal generational mark-and-sweep garbage collection engine.

diffs.from: Native multi-core concurrency using system threads, atomics, and low-level synchronization variables.
diffs.to: Thread abstractions constrained historically by a Global VM Lock (GVL), supplemented by modern Ractors and Fibers.

diffs.from: The STL, which provides precise, performance-tuned system algorithms and collection containers.
diffs.to: Rich object-oriented collections tracking sophisticated regex pipelines, system file interactions, and web string tools.

examples.title: File Streaming Iteration
examples.description: Simplifying verbose structural file loops into an elegant block iteration pattern.
examples.toCode:
def read_file
  File.foreach('data.txt') do |line|
    puts line
  end
end

examples.title: Dynamic Vector Array Management
examples.description: Replacing rigid vector collections with dynamic auto-resizing array objects.
examples.toCode:
# Arrays dynamically track capacity natively
nums = [10, 20]
nums << 30
puts nums.length

tips.title: Treat Everything as a Concrete Object
tips.body: Discard primitive scalar mindsets. In Ruby, literal numbers (like `100`), text strings, and full class models are concrete object instances that expose executable methods.

tips.title: Leverage Dynamic Metaprogramming Capabilities
tips.body: Instead of writing heavy C++ compile-time templates, use Ruby tools like `define_method` or `method_missing` to safely modify object structures at runtime.

tips.title: Understand the Limits of the Global VM Lock
tips.body: Be aware that standard Ruby code patterns are managed under a GVL framework, which limits concurrent CPU-bound instruction streams on multi-core systems.

tips.title: Expose Performance Critical Blocks via C Extensions
tips.body: If specific algorithmic operations hit runtime speed ceilings, wrap those performance-critical segments in custom native C/C++ extensions to run them inside your Ruby gems.
